title: Hand carding rolags
subtitle: Preparing raw or washed fleece into fluffy rolags for woollen spinning using hand cards
excerpt: Hand carders are a pair of paddle-shaped tools with fine wire teeth used to open, align, and blend fleece fibres before spinning. The process produces a rolag: a loose cylindrical roll of prepared fibre ready for the woollen draw on a wheel or spindle. Hand cards are more portable and slower than a drum carder but give the spinner more control over small quantities and fibre blends.
sourceNotes: Hand-carding instruction is foundational to spinning education across UK guilds and has been documented in spinning manuals since the 18th century. Method draws on the standard double-card technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A loose cylindrical roll of hand-carded fibre. The fibres in a rolag are arranged in a helical pattern around the roll rather than parallel along its length. This preparation is suited to the woollen draw, which produces a lofty, airy yarn with good insulation.

attrs.title: Wool dust
attrs.body: Carding throws fine fibre dust. Work in a ventilated room and damp-mop after. Wear a dust mask if you have asthma.

content.text: prepared by hand carding has a different fibre structure from combed top: the fibres spiral around the roll rather than running parallel along it. When the spinner drafts from a rolag, this structure creates the characteristic loftiness of woollen-spun yarn. The same fleece, combed rather than carded, produces a denser, smoother worsted yarn.

items.name: Washed and dried fleece, any breed suitable for carding (Corriedale, BFL, Polwarth)
Pair of hand carders (fine or medium tooth depending on staple length)

content.text: Hold one carder (the left card) teeth upward on your knee. Take a small handful of fleece (about 5-8 g) and draw it lightly across the teeth from the handle end toward the far edge of the card. Repeat until the card surface is lightly loaded with an even layer of fleece. Do not overload: a thin, even layer cards faster and more cleanly than a thick mat.

Hold the left card still (teeth upward, handle toward you). Take the right card (teeth facing down) and stroke it lightly across the left card in the direction the teeth point, from the handle end of the left card toward its far edge. Use a light brushing stroke rather than a pressing stroke: you are untangling fibres, not forcing them through the teeth. Transfer fibres from one card to the other by reversing direction: hold the right card still and stroke the left card across it. Repeat 5-8 times until the fibres are open, untangled, and evenly distributed across both cards.

Transfer all the carded fibre to the right card. Lay the right card teeth-up on your knee with the handle away from you. Place a pencil or large knitting needle at the far edge of the card. Roll the fibre off the card teeth by rolling it toward you around the pencil, forming a loose cylinder. Slide the pencil out. The cylinder, called a rolag, should be loose and airy: do not compress it.

items.problem: The carded fibre mats into the card teeth and will not transfer
items.cause: Card overloaded with too much fleece, or fleece too dirty or too short
items.fix: Load smaller amounts. Clean the cards by combing out the accumulated fibre with a carder-cleaner brush after every 5-6 rolags.

items.problem: The rolag is dense and mat-like rather than light and airy
items.cause: Over-carding or pressing too hard during the carding stroke
items.fix: Use a lighter stroke and stop carding when the fibres are just untangled, not when they are fully parallel. Carding is for opening, not for aligning.

items.problem: The finished rolag has noticeable lumps of undrafted fibre
items.cause: The fleece was not sorted well enough before carding; vegetable matter or matted locks slipped through
items.fix: Pick over the fleece carefully before loading the card. Discard any matted locks: they do not card out and produce thick places in the yarn.
